text.title: Terraform Advanced Pre-Deployment Checklist
text.summary: State architecture, module quality, CI/CD pipeline, security hardening, and production deployment checklists.

rule.statement: Verify: separate state files per environment AND per component; remote backend with locking and encryption; .terraform.lock.hcl committed; no state files in git; provider versions pinned.
rule.rationale: These five checks together eliminate the most common Terraform production incidents: state corruption, credential leaks, provider version drift, and blast radius from monolithic state.

rule.statement: Confirm blast-radius isolation: a destroy in one environment's state cannot affect another environment; component-level states minimize resources affected per apply.
rule.rationale: Without isolation, terraform destroy on the networking state of prod takes down the entire production network. Component isolation limits each operation's scope.

rule.statement: Verify pipeline has all stages: fmt -check, validate, tflint, tfsec/checkov, plan -out=tfplan, required human approval for prod, apply tfplan (not re-plan), post-apply health checks.
rule.rationale: Each missing stage is a class of error that reaches production without detection. The saved-plan requirement is the most commonly missing critical control.

rule.statement: Confirm: OIDC authentication (no static keys), TF_IN_AUTOMATION=true, plan artifact stored for audit, apply output logged, Dependabot/Renovate for controlled provider/module updates.
rule.rationale: Static keys are a credential leak risk. Plan artifacts provide the audit trail for change management. Automated dependency updates prevent security vulnerabilities from accumulating.

rule.statement: Verify: no hardcoded secrets in .tf files, all sensitive variables marked sensitive = true, all storage resources have encryption enabled, no 0.0.0.0/0 security group rules without justification, IAM follows least privilege.
rule.rationale: Static analysis (tfsec/checkov) catches the common cases, but manual review is needed for business-logic-specific IAM policies that scanners cannot evaluate.

rule.statement: Before prod apply: review plan for unexpected destroys or replacements; assess cost impact (Infracost); create change management ticket; document rollback procedure; notify team.
rule.rationale: Unexpected destroys in plan output are the most common sign of an unintended infrastructure change. A reviewed plan with no surprises is the primary safety control for production Terraform.

rule.statement: After prod apply: run health checks and smoke tests; verify monitoring alerts are active; run terraform plan again to confirm clean state (no drift).
rule.rationale: A second clean plan after apply confirms the deployed state matches configuration exactly. Drift between apply runs indicates an out-of-band change or Terraform bug.
